title: System for the detection of paralytic shellfish poisoning related toxins based on genetically modified microorganisms
abstract: The present invention relates to a method and biosensor system for a quick and sensitive detection of toxins related to Paralytic Shellfish Poisoning (PSP) or to Harmful Algae Blooms (HABs), a phenomenon colloquially known as red tide. The present invention also concerns obtaining genetically modified microorganisms, such as bacteria, yeast, neuronal cell lines, cardiovascular cell lines, or any other cell that contains at least one detection route serving as a toxicological effect target. Such detection route is capable of sensing its environment and providing a response or signal corresponding to the presence or absence of PSP or HAB related marine biotoxins or their associated toxic metabolites. Thus, the application field of the present invention belongs in biotechnology techniques used to provide methods and biosensors for environmental metabolites that are toxic to human health.
claims: Claims What is claimed is: 1 . A biosensor system for detection of Paralytic Shellfish Poisoning (PSP) related toxins, comprising: a) a first component consisting of at least one genetically modified microorganism, that contains at least one DNA construct for sensing osmotic stress comprising a promoter sequence and a reporter gene; b) a second component consisting of at least one genetically modified microorganism, that contains at least one DNA construct for sensing oxidative stress comprising a promoter sequence and a reporter gene; wherein the genetically modified microorganisms, for sensing osmotic stress construct and oxidative stress reacts to the presence of PSP related toxins through expression of at least one reporter gene. 2. The biosensor system of claim 1 , wherein the first component having at least one DNA construct for sensing osmotic stress has a promoter sequence selected from osmB, spr, yehZ, osmE, osmC, among others. 3. The biosensor system of claim 1 , wherein the second component having at least one DNA construct for sensing oxidative stress has a promoter sequence selected from ygjG, ybgS, ybgS, tktB, lysR, talA, pdhR, dps, gadW, hdhA, gadB, tarn, psiF, sufl, narU, ycaC, rssA, yhhT, yncG, yncG, mscL, grxA, hdeD, osmE, yiaG, bolA, sip, wrbA, yjbJ, yjeB, chaB, oxyR, ompC, ahpC, ahpF, among others. 4. The biosensor system of claim 1 , wherein both first and second components having at least one DNA construct have a reporter gene selected from eGFP, GFP, luciferase, amilCP among others. 5. The biosensor system of claim 1 , wherein the at least one genetically modified microorganism is selected from as bacteria, yeast, neuronal cell lines, cardiovascular cell lines, or any other cell suitable for genetic transformation. 6. The biosensor system of claiml , wherein the biosensor system also comprises an optional a third component consisting of at least one genetically modified microorganism that contains at least one DNA construct for sensing cooper fluxes comprising a promoter sequence and a reporter gene. 7. The biosensor system of claim 6, wherein the third component having at least one DNA construct for sensing cooper fluxes has a promoter sequence selected from cooper flux membrane proteins. 8. The biosensor system of claim 6, wherein the third component having at least one DNA construct have a reporter gene selected from eGFP, GFP, luciferase, amilCP among others. 9. The biosensor system of claims 1 or 6, wherein any of the at least one DNA construct for sensing for sensing osmotic stress, oxidative stress and/or or cooper fluxes, is comprised in the same single microorganism.
